title: Health worker being monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC
summary: UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease
body: A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential exposure to the Ebola virus. The UK resident was medically evacuated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said. UKHSA said the person was evacuated as a “highly precautionary measure”, adding: “The individual is not currently displaying any symptoms and remains well, but as a precautionary measure they have been evacuated on a flight chartered solely for the evacuation. “They are now being assessed and monitored in isolation by infectious disease specialists at a London hospital.” The agency said the incubation period of Ebola ranges from two to 21 days and the individual will be closely monitored and is required to isolate throughout this period. There are no confirmed cases of Ebola in the UK and the risk to the general public remains low. Richard Pebody, director of epidemic and emerging infections at the UKHSA, said: “This individual has been transferred out of an abundance of caution and we’re pleased they remain well.” Last month, a patient suspected of having the Ebola virus at a Glasgow hospital tested negative. Precautionary measures were taken after the patient was admitted to the Queen Elizabeth University hospital. An outbreak of the disease in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda in May was declared a public health emergency of international concern by the World Health Organization (WHO). The virus is transmitted to people from wild animals and then spreads in the human population through direct contact with the blood, secretions, organs or other bodily fluids of infected people and with contaminated surfaces and materials such as bedding or clothing. According to WHO, symptoms can include fever, fatigue, malaise, muscle, pain, headache and sore throat. These are followed by vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, rash, and symptoms of impaired kidney and liver functions.

title: Iran war has cost $37.5bn so far, Hegseth tells Senate – US politics live
summary: Protesters removed at start of testimony as defense secretary claims billions more in funding is needed ‘sustain vital operations without disruption’
body: The US-Israeli war on Iran has cost the Pentagon $37.5bn so far, defense secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday. At the Senate hearing, which has now concluded, senator Dick Durbin, an Illinois Democrat, asked Hegseth to explain why the Pentagon was asking Congress for an additional $67bn to pay for the war Trump started on 28 February when “the Department of Defense still has $75bn in unobligated funding from last year’s reconciliation package”. “That 75,” Hegseth replied, “there’s plans and designs on that part of the rebuild of our military which had to be undertaken.” Hegseth then launched into a deeply partisan attack on his predecessor, Lloyd Austin, the retired army general, for supposedly pursuing “woke priorities” instead of military readiness. “I don’t know what Lloyd Austin did for four years, I really don’t,” he went on. “The amount of neglect, the amount of stale thinking and bureaucracy proceed staggering when we took over.” “Let me interrupt your speech for a minute, Mr Secretary,” Durbin cut in. “You estimated at one point that the cost of the war in Iran was $29bn, but then added a caveat that there was a replacement of munitions, for example. Do you have a new estimate about what the war has cost us so far in this the fifth month of the first year of this war in Iran?” the senator asked. “Senator, the estimate we have, as of today, is $37.5bn,” Hegseth answered. Pressed further by Durbin, Hegseth said that was the estimated cost of the war on Iran through the end of the US government’s financial year on 30 September. The White House called on Tuesday for the House and Senate to pass the Republican SAVE America Act, which would require proof of citizenship to register to vote, and photo ID to cast a ballot, after New Jersey’s governor, Mikie Sherrill, revealed that a computer software error in the state’s automatic voter registration system for licensed drivers mistakenly registered about 6,600 people who identified themselves as non-US citizens in 2023 and 2024. According to Sherrill, who was not in office at the time, “fewer than 400 individuals who were newly registered for the first time because of the error voted. They were registered as Democrats, Republicans and Unaffiliated voters and were scattered across the state.” David Becker, a voting rights expert who leads the nonprofit Center for Election Innovation & Research, put the size of the glitch into context in a statement sent to reporters. “While administrative errors such as New Jersey’s do infrequently occur, they are very rare and affect a very small fraction of voters. The states entrusted to run our elections are best positioned to detect and correct these errors, as they have access to substantially better data than the federal government,” Becker said. “As CEIR’s own research affirms, noncitizen registration remains extremely rare, and noncitizen voting is infinitesimally rare. In this case, the number of wrongful registrations represents a miniscule fraction, roughly 0.1%, of New Jersey’s total active voter registration records in 2024,” he continued. “The number of potential ballots cast from those incorrect registrations would represent roughly 0.009% of all ballots cast in New Jersey’s 2024 general election, when Kamala Harris defeated President Trump by more than 250,000 votes,” Becker added. “Joe Biden actually defeated Trump by a margin three times as large before this error took place.” At the ongoing Senate Appropriations Committee hearing, senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, pressed US defense secretary Pete Hegseth to say whether the US military could bomb a fortified underground site linked to Iran’s nuclear program known as Pickaxe mountain, which the US has not yet targeted. “We need straight answers,” Kennedy said with some exasperation. “Do we have the ability to destroy whatever is underneath Pickaxe mountain?” “Senator, a lot of our capabilities are classified, but if anybody in the world can reach anything on God’s given, God’s green Earth, it’s the United States military,” Hegseth replied. Kennedy’s question was perhaps triggered by reports that experts suggest the site is beyond the reach of the most powerful bunker ⁠buster bombs in the US arsenal. Donald Trump was asked by a reporter earlier on Tuesday if he believes an Israeli intelligence assessment that Iran has moved nuclear centrifuges into tunnels deep inside the mountain, called Kolang Gaz La in Persian, which is about a mile from Iran’s badly damaged ⁠Natanz uranium enrichment facility. “I think that they may have; we don’t have it on record,” Trump replied, before veering into a digression to cast the speculation about the mountain as possibly flawed reporting from news outlets. “All we do is read the fake news, you know, we see… the news is so wrong so many times, so you know, maybe this one they have right.” “It doesn’t mean anything unless they have the material. You’re talking about the centrifuges,” Trump added. “We follow the material, that’s where the action is, and we’ll be hitting that area very, probably pretty soon and very heavily.” According to the nonpartisan Institute for Science and International Security think tank in Washington, this site was “not visibly attacked” by US bombers in 2025, when US bombers dropped two two GBU-57 bunker buster bombs on the nearby nuclear facility at Natanz. “Iran announced in 2020 that the purpose of the Pickaxe tunnel complex is to hold an advanced centrifuge assembly facility that can produce several thousand centrifuges a year,” the institute reported last year. “In addition, based on the estimated size of the underground halls, the Institute several years ago assessed that site could also hold a centrifuge enrichment facility.” The Senate intelligence committee on Tuesday advanced the nomination of Jay Clayton to serve as the US intelligence director, despite criticism from Democrats. Donald Trump had nominated Clayton, now the top federal prosecutor in the southern district of New York, for the role of the director of national intelligence (DNI) last month after the departure of Tulsi Gabbard. Intelligence committee members questioned Clayton at a hearing last week, and its Republican chair, Tom Cotton, announced that it had reported his nomination favorably by a 9-8 vote, teeing him up for a future vote on his confirmation by the full Senate. “Jay Clayton is a highly qualified nominee with a deep experience combatting a wide range of national security threats,” Cotton said in a statement. “I’m pleased that the Senate Intelligence Committee voted to advance his nomination to be Director of National Intelligence,” he added. Cotton did not provide a breakdown of how lawmakers voted. The committee is composed of nine Republicans and eight Democrats, and a spokesperson for the Democratic vice-chair, Mark Warner, said he had opposed the nomination. At his hearing last week, members of the Democratic minority criticized Clayton, a former Wall Street attorney who served as chair of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), for refusing to say that Joe Biden won the 2020 election. He also faced questions about previous statements he made echoing Trump’s claim that US elections have been marred by fraud, and concerns about politicization at the office of the DNI, which serves to coordinate the nation’s intelligence agencies. At the Senate Appropriations Committee hearing, Dan Caine, the chairman of the joint chiefs of staff, was asked by senator Jack Reed, a Rhode Island Democrat and veteran who previously taught economics and international relations at West Point, whether Iran can be deterred and defeated by “air strikes alone”. “I think, academically, as you look across the totality of history, air power’s got limits, and we always have to be mindful of those,” Caine responded. He went on to say that the US is combing military power with “the totality of the diplomatic, informational and economic” measures. “There’s a loot of that work going on, sir,” Trump’s hand-picked joint chiefs chair added. Reed then asked if Caine anticipates the next step in escalation: ground troops. “Sir, I think it would be unhelpful for me to opine on hypotheticals,” Caine replied. He added that the Pentagon’s job was to develop military options, and understand the risks associated with them. “My main job is to always ensure that leaders understand those options and the risks,” Caine added. Hegseth has yet to make reference to the deaths of the three US service members last week in the Iran conflict. In his opening remarks Dan Caine, the chairman of the joint chiefs of staff, asked the senators and American public to “never forget our fallen and their families, who continue to show us what courage, tenacity, grit, and commitment to the nation looks like”. The defense secretary’s remarks come despite the fact that both chambers have passed a war powers resolution that urges the president to seek congressional authorization to continue military operations in Iran. While answering questions by Republican senator Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, Hegseth said that his department thinks “standalone Article II authorities exist for us to conduct this operation on behalf of the defense of the American people”. The defense secretary evaded the lawmaker’s question about whether he would use the approval of any supplemental funding as a “substitute for explicit congressional authorization”. Hegseth responded to a question from Senator Susan Collins – the chair of the appropriations committee – about the impact on military exercises if the supplemental funding is not provided. The defense secretary said that current and future training “would have to be curtailed” if the Pentagon does not get its budget needs met urgently. “This $1.5tn dollar budget with this supplemental being part of it is how we catch up and get ahead, and ensure in the process we’re putting soldiers and sailors and…

title: Trump pledges to ‘take care of’ Houthis if group blocks Saudi ships and says US airlines can resume direct flights to Lebanon
summary: The Iran-based group said it plans to impose an immediate maritime blockade of Saudi Arabia in the Red Sea
body: In case you’re just joining us, here’s the main developments to know: US forces bombed targets in the south and west of Iran overnight, while Iran targeted US sites in Bahrain, Kuwait ⁠and Jordan and a tanker was hit in ⁠the strait of Hormuz at the mouth of the Gulf. Two oil tankers carrying Saudi crude to Asia reversed course in the Red Sea on Tuesday after threats from Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthis. Donald Trump said the Houthis had not yet shut the Bab el-Mandeb, the “Gate of Tears” strait leading ⁠into the Red Sea, and threatened to act against them if they did. “So far it hasn’t happened,” Trump said. “If something like that happens, we take care of it.” Trump renewed his threats to again attack Iran’s nuclear facilities at Natanz “pretty soon“, which he said in June 2025 had been “totally obliterated” after the US military bombed the facility. Defense secretary Pete Hegseth faced questions over the war with Iran, which he estimated has cost $37.5bn so far, as Republicans prepare a $95bn budget package to fund the military, along with other White House priorities. Trump pledged to help Lebanon “a lot” during a White House meeting with the country’s leader, Joseph Aoun. Trump made the surprise announcement that he’d allow American carriers to resume direct flights to Lebanon for the first time in 40-plus years after the US suspended the route. Oil prices gained more than 2% on Tuesday, with Brent crude hovering above $91 a barrel. The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, walked into the Senate on Tuesday to defend the Trump administration’s $87.6bn supplemental funding request, facing lawmakers furious that the US-Israel war on Iran is costing far more than the White House has admitted. Hegseth and the chair of the joint chiefs, Gen Dan Caine, faced the Senate appropriations committee, chaired by Susan Collins of Maine, as the Pentagon said it was running low on cash to keep the fight going. Of the total request, $67.1bn of the request is earmarked specifically to keep the war effort funded. It was the pair’s first sustained public questioning since a brief ceasefire collapsed and the war roared back to life. At least two US soldiers were killed in an Iranian attack on Friday, and another died in Iraq destroying an Iranian drone, pushing the American death toll since February to 17. Nearly 100 troops have been wounded in the conflict, officials confirm, though most injuries were minor. Iran’s top joint military command said it would target the interests of the US and its allies if the American military targeted Iranian nuclear sites, Iran’s state media reported. The statement by Iran’s Khatem al-Anbiya Central Headquarters comes after Donald Trump said the US will be hitting the Pickaxe Mountain area “pretty soon”. Lebanese president Joseph Aoun told Donald Trump a full Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory was essential to consolidating stability and enabling the state to extend its authority, the Lebanese presidency said. In their first meeting, the two leaders discussed plans to disarm the Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah and secure Israel’s withdrawal from Lebanon. The Lebanese presidency said they also discussed US support for Lebanon’s army, reconstruction, economic recovery and American investment. In a statement after the talks, the Lebanese presidency said Aoun stressed to Trump the “urgent need for a complete Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory,” calling it “an essential step to consolidate stability and enable the Lebanese state to extend its full sovereignty exclusively through its own forces.” Yemen’s Iranian-aligned Houthis have warned shipping companies that their tankers risk being attacked if they use Saudi Arabian ports, as the militia moved quickly to enforce a blockade announced the previous day. The Houthi warning for the Red Sea, coming on top of Iran’s threat to shipping on the other side of the Arabian peninsular in the Gulf, could affect the substantial Saudi oil exports that have so far been unscathed by the months-long conflict and deepen the rapidly escalating crisis in the region. Underlining how seriously the Houthi threat is being taken, two oil tankers that had loaded Saudi crude ⁠this week for China and India made U-turns on Tuesday away from Houthi-controlled coast at the mouth of the Red Sea and headed towards the Suez canal. The Houthis claimed six ships had changed course. The US’s war in Iran has cost $37.5bn so far, defense secretary Pete Hegseth said, an increase of nearly $8bn since the last public estimate. Hegseth told lawmakers the figure also included anticipated costs through 30 September. Donald Trump said he will be directing his administration to “allow all US airline carriers to fly directly to Lebanon so that Americans can easily visit this beautiful land.” Trump’s announcement, which he made on Truth Social, came after his meeting with Lebanese president Joseph Aoun earlier on Tuesday at the White House. Since 1985, there have been no direct flights between the US and Lebanon after then US president Ronald Reagan suspended air service following the hijacking of TWA Flight 847. During the meeting, Trump said he plans to help Lebanon “a lot,” describing it as a “a very badly treated place and country and we’re going to have it properly treated and treated with the respect it deserves.” Hegseth also said that Iran is “at the weakest point militarily it has ever been in the past decade.” He added: “I acknowledge they still have capabilities, no doubt, but the amount of damage that we’ve done to them over the course of these series of operations has put them in the worst place they’ve ever [been].” In response to a question from Washington senator Patty Murray on how Iran was able to shrink ship traffic across the Hormuz Strait last week despite Hegseth previously claiming that Iran does not control the strait, Hegseth said: “We pushed almost, I believe, between 400 and 500 million barrels of oil onto the nose of Iran over the course of Project Freedom, which went underground, which I acknowledged after we did so covertly because it was more effective to do so quietly than to do so publicly. So we’ve been in control of moving traffic through there for quite some time.” In response to whether the Donald Trump administration still holds the position that it does not require authorization from Congress for the US’s war on Iran, US secretary of defense Pete Hegseth said: “I know there’s a long longstanding legal debate on this particular topic, but we certainly, as a department, share the position of the White House that we have all the authorities necessary at this moment.” US defense secretary Pete Hegseth and chairman of the joint chief of staff, General Dan Caine, are appearing before the US Congress to ask for additional money for the US’s war on Iran. The defense department is set to request approximately $80 billion for the US’s military operations across Iran, the New York Times reports. In total, Hegseth told Congress that “$1.5 trillion is what the president seeks and what we need to defend the country.” “The members of this committee understand better than most that long-term strategic dominance cannot be funded through one-off supplements,” he told the senate appropriations committee. Kuwait’s foreign ministry has summoned Iran’s ambassador Muhammad Tutunji where “he was handed a protest note regarding Iran’s targeting of the Kuwaiti tanker ‘Kifan,’ on Monday evening,” as it passed through the Hormuz Strait. In a statement on X, the foreign ministry said that Kuwait’s deputy foreign minister Hamad Sulaiman Al-Mashaan “affirmed that this brazen aggression comes amid the escalation of the heinous attacks” that Iran has launched against the country since February. It added that the attack “constitutes a flagrant violation of the sovereignty of the state of Kuwait.” “The state of Kuwait renewed its demand for Iran to immediately cease its illegitimate attacks, emphasizing Kuwait’s reservation of its right to take necessary measures, and holding Iran fully responsible for this aggression and its ongoing assaults against the state of Kuwait,” the ministry continued. Alarm sirens have been activated across Bahrain, the country’s interior ministry said on Tuesday evening. “Citizens and residents are requested to remain calm, head to the nearest safe location, and follow updates through official channels,” it said on X. Andrew Roth and Sufian Taha Even by the brutal standards of militant settler violence in the West Bank, the raid on Deir Jarir late on Sunday night that killed two Palestinians and left another in hospital with a gunshot wound was extreme. Palestinians in the town said settlers from a nearby outpost arrived after 9pm on Sunday claiming that they were searching for stolen livestock. Israeli soldiers and police soon joined them, protecting them as they sought to take several villagers’ sheep by force. But when villagers raised the alarm and a crowd gathered, soldiers shot two men, beginning a night of three successive raids by settlers from nearby outposts and Israeli soldiers that lasted until after 2am, according to Palestinian residents of Deir Jarir. Saber Ajaj, a relative of one of the victims, 53-year-old Odeh Farajna, said he found him moments after he had been shot through the chest. Farajna, a member of the village council, owned a flower shop and helped with the stonework at the local graveyard. US president Donald Trump said that the United States will continue attacks on Iran, while forecasting it would take the country more than 20 years to rebuild from current damage. “If we left right now, it would take Iran 20, 25 years to rebuild. Okay, and we’re not finished at all...we’re not leaving right now,” he told reporters during a White House meeting with Lebanese president Joseph Aoun. Aoun urged Trump to maintain American support for his country’s military, warning that southern Lebanon could see a return of the chaos of…

title: Bay Area city manager charged for allegedly breaking into 66ft yacht while drinking
summary: Sausalito city manager Elaine Forbes faces misdemeanor charges for allegedly trying to steal the three-story vessel
body: A Bay Area city manager will face misdemeanor charges for allegedly breaking into a 66ft yacht while drinking and trying to steal the vessel. Elaine Forbes, Sausalito’s city manager, was arrested on the morning of 18 July after police received a call for a noise disturbance. Police say Forbes had “apparently broken into” a three-story yacht, where she was consuming alcohol. Forbes, who previously served as director of the Port of San Francisco, appeared to make a ruckus on the yacht. The boat’s owner, Andy Cunningham, told ABC7 that she found a bottle of maraschino cherries dumped into a shoebox and four empty bottles of wine. “She had the horn going, the windshield wipers going, the spotlight going,” Capt Bill Aubrey, who called 911 on Forbes, told ABC7. Forbes had been living on a houseboat near the marina where she was arrested, ABC7 reported. Police initially charged Forbes with felony burglary, and her bail was set at $50,000. But prosecutors have since lessened those to misdemeanor charges for loitering, squatting and petty theft, according to Sgt Adam Schermerhorn, a spokesperson for the Marin county sheriff’s department. Yacht theft is not common in Marin county, he said. The Sausalito city council had placed Forbes on leave last week during a closed meeting, according to NBC News. The city council is evaluating her job performance at a meeting today. She was appointed to her position three weeks ago. “As the facts become clearer and Elaine obtains legal and healthcare counsel in the coming hours and days, we hope people will respond with compassion, patience and respect for her and her family during an extraordinarily difficult time,” spokesperson Sam Singer said in a widely circulated statement issued on behalf of the Forbes family.

title: Zelenskyy fires Ukraine’s army chief and offers Fedorov ‘prominent position’
summary: After nationwide protests Oleksandr Syrskyi is to be replaced in reshuffle and popular defence minister returns to government
body: Volodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests. In a late-night TV address, Zelenskyy said he was appointing Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular and innovative general, as the new head of Ukraine’s armed forces. Drapatyi would oversee a reorganisation of the army’s command structure, Ukraine’s president said. Zelenskyy said he was bringing Mykhailo Fedorov back into government after his removal as defence minister prompted thousands of people to demonstrate in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, in one of the biggest political crises since Russia’s 2022 invasion. Ukraine’s president paid tribute to Syrskyi and credited him with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024, in which foreign tanks rolled on to Russian territory for the first time since the second world war. “We have come a long way. The defence of Ukraine continues. And every soldier deserves to be treated with respect,” Zelenskyy said, adding that he was grateful to Syrskyi and to “every one of our soldiers” fighting on the frontline. Zelenskyy will be hoping that his radical reshuffle of the army’s leadership will assuage protesters who have taken to the streets demanding Syrskyi’s removal. They had given a deadline of Friday for Fedorov to be reinstated and promised “indefinite protests” if their request was ignored. Fedorov welcomed Drapatyi’s appointment. “This brings a breath of fresh air and new hope to the struggle of free people for freedom and justice. It is a voice for change that could not be ignored,” he said. He added: “The high expectations of the Ukrainian people must be met with a clear vision for ending the war for independence, a strong team and decisive actions aimed at saving the lives of our soldiers, automating the frontline, delivering asymmetric strikes and crippling the Russian economy.” Drapatyi, the new commander-in-chief, is well-regarded within Ukraine’s army and is liked by the public. He is viewed as an ally of Fedorov, whose attempts to reform the military and force it to adopt modern technology and data led to a bitter clash with the old-school Syrskyi. A major general, Drapatyi is commander of Ukraine’s joint forces group. He has been fighting Russia since its covert attempt in 2014 to take over the eastern Donbas region. He became famous nationally when he drove an infantry fighting vehicle over a barricade set up by pro-Russian militants in the city of Mariupol. The Ukrainian media outlet Babel described Drapatyi as one of Ukraine’s most capable battlefield commanders. It said his new job would be fundamentally about reforming a large institution rather than commanding troops in combat. Zelenskyy said he and Drapatyi agreed that Ukraine’s long-range drone attacks on targets in Russia such as oil refineries would continue. “Our programme of precision strikes will be implemented with absolute precision,” he said, promising a smooth and coordinated handover following Syrskyi’s departure. He also said the new commander-in-chief would be tasked with updating the country’s defence strategy and reforming its structure. Another pressing issue is mobilisation, at a time when after nearly five years of full-scale war few Ukrainians are ready to volunteer. It is unclear what Fedorov’s new role will be, amid reports that he was insisting on being given his old job back as defence minister. This has now been filled by Yevhen Khmara, a former special operations commander. Zelenskyy said he met Fedorov on Tuesday. “I offered Mykhailo a prominent position within the government that would enable him to unify the technological sector of our state and ensure its development,” he said, without giving further details. Zelenskyy added: “Our shared wish is one – victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace.” He said “a significant path has been travelled, the defence of Ukraine continues, and every warrior deserves dignified treatment”.

title: Senate committee advances nomination of Trump intelligence pick Jay Clayton
summary: Committee voted 9-8 to advance Clayton’s nomination to serve as intelligence chief despite Democratic opposition
body: The Senate intelligence committee on Tuesday advanced the nomination of Jay Clayton to serve as the US intelligence director, despite criticism from Democrats. Donald Trump had nominated Clayton, now the top federal prosecutor in the southern district of New York, for the role of the director of national intelligence (DNI) last month after the departure of Tulsi Gabbard. Intelligence committee members questioned Clayton at a hearing last week, and its Republican chair, Tom Cotton, announced that it had reported his nomination favorably by a 9-8 vote, teeing him up for a future vote on his confirmation by the full Senate. “Jay Clayton is a highly qualified nominee with a deep experience combatting a wide range of national security threats,” Cotton said in a statement. “I’m pleased that the Senate Intelligence Committee voted to advance his nomination to be Director of National Intelligence,” he added. Cotton did not provide a breakdown of how lawmakers voted. The committee is composed of nine Republicans and eight Democrats, and Mark Warner, a spokesperson for the Democratic vice-chair, said he had opposed the nomination. Democratic senator Ron Wyden released a statement announcing his no vote, accusing Clayton of “spreading conspiracies” about mail-in voting and denying Joe Biden’s victory in the 2020 election. “From start to finish, Mr Clayton has proven himself incapable of telling the truth or demonstrating an iota of independence from Donald Trump,” Wyden said. At his hearing last week, members of the Democratic minority criticized Clayton, a former Wall Street attorney who served as the chair of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), for previous statements he made echoing Trump’s claim that US elections have been marred by fraud. They also aired concerns about politicization at the office of the DNI, which serves to coordinate the nation’s intelligence agencies. It is unclear when his nomination will be voted on by the Senate. If confirmed, he would take over from Bill Pulte, a federal housing official who Trump appointed as acting DNI following Gabbard’s departure. Democrats strenuously objected to Pulte’s installation in that office, decrying him as a loyalist to the president who may try to use its powers to retaliate against his enemies. As director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency director, Pulte had last year referred several Democratic officeholders as well as the Federal Reserve governor Lisa Cook for mortgage fraud. His predecessor, Gabbard, had raised similar concerns when she appeared alongside FBI agents who raided an election office in Georgia earlier this year. Administration officials later said she was running her own investigation of the 2020 election. Clayton’s confirmation may nonetheless clear a path for Congress to renew the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (Fisa), a key spy tool that had its legal authorization expire last month in part due to a blockade by Democrats incensed by Trump’s appointment of Pulte. Since taking over as acting DNI, Pulte has reportedly fired dozens of staffers from the office. In his statement, Cotton signaled support for those purges, saying he looked forward to working with Clayton to “continue reforming the Office of Director of National Intelligence”.

title: Bill Gates foundation met with Jeffrey Epstein about 30 times, external review finds
summary: Staff ‘raised on multiple occasions’ risks of tech mogul’s philanthropic body associating with disgraced financier
body: A new external review commissioned by the Gates Foundation has found that there were “roughly 30 meetings” between Jeffrey Epstein and foundation leaders and staff – including Bill Gates – between 2011 and 2014, even as foundation staff “raised on multiple occasions the risks of associating with Epstein because of his prior conviction”. The Gates foundation commissioned the review in March, following the justice department’s release earlier this year of millions of documents related to Epstein. The files included numerous references to Gates, several photographs of him, and records showing that he met with Epstein on multiple occasions, which prompted renewed scrutiny of Gates’s past ties to the disgraced financier. The foundation also said earlier this year that it was aware of emails in the Epstein files “between Jeffrey Epstein and Gates Foundation staff”. The foundation said, at the time, that it “regrets having any employees interact with Epstein in any way” and retained the law firm WilmerHale to conduct a review of the foundation’s past engagements with Epstein. The report, of which a summary was released on Tuesday, took about five months. It was based on more than 50 interviews with current and former foundation personnel, including Gates, and reviewed extensive written materials relevant to the review, the law firm said. “WilmerHale found no evidence of participation in or knowledge of Epstein’s ongoing sex trafficking operations or criminal activities,” the summary of the report states. “WilmerHale found no evidence that the foundation was contemporaneously aware of Epstein’s use of the foundation’s name to help advance his conduct. WilmerHale also found no evidence that Epstein was paid by the foundation.” Gates, who has not been accused of any wrongdoing in connection with Epstein and has repeatedly denied having any knowledge of Epstein’s crimes, has publicly expressed regret over his past association with Epstein. He apologized to foundation employees during a February town-hall meeting for his past ties to Epstein, the Wall Street Journal reported. In June, he testified in front of the House committee on oversight and reform and told lawmakers that he “never witnessed nor had any indication” that Epstein was “engaged in ongoing criminal conduct”. The Microsoft co-founder said that he was introduced to Epstein in 2011 “through people I trusted in my professional and philanthropic work” and that Epstein had “claimed he could raise billions of dollars for global health from people for whom he provided tax and estate services”. “I recall being aware that Epstein had faced prior legal issues, but I did not fully understand the extent of the crimes he committed,” Gates said, referring to Epstein having pleaded guilty in 2008 to state charges in Florida of solicitation of prostitution and of solicitation of prostitution with a minor. “I accepted the introduction without applying the scrutiny I should have.” Gates said that their interactions ended in December 2014. According to the new report, Epstein was introduced to Gates and other foundation personnel in 2011 “by an adviser who held a dual role at the foundation and in Mr Gates’s personal office”. The review found that the foundation’s interactions with Epstein involved two matters – discussions about the “potential creation of a donor-advised fund entity” and Gates’s “engagement with a nonprofit organization that later became a foundation grantee”. WilmerHale said that it identified “roughly 30 meetings between Epstein and ten foundation leaders and staff, including Mr Gates, from 2011 to 2014” including one meeting on the foundation’s campus and several meetings at Epstein’s Manhattan residence. “The foundation’s meetings with Epstein appear to have focused on the DAF concept, donor engagement, the foundation’s polio strategy, and introductions to IPI,” the summary of the report states. “Based on interviews and a review of available foundation records, WilmerHale found no evidence that these meetings were unrelated to philanthropy or involved illegal conduct.” The report also concluded that Gates “was aware of reputational concerns related to Epstein’s prior conviction and sentencing for a sex-related offense when he began conversations with him in 2011” and that foundation staff had “raised on multiple occasions the risks of associating with Epstein because of his prior conviction, and those concerns were raised to senior leadership, including Mr. Gates”. In a news release on Tuesday, the Gates Foundation said that its governing board had approved a series of recommendations based on findings in the report, including creating a centralized vetting process for brokers, donor advisers, co-funders and others who facilitate funding partnerships and who are connected to the foundation, as well as creating an “escalation process for organizational risks on projects” and more. Gates said in a statement on Tuesday that “completing this review is an important step in providing the clarity that partners, employees, and grantees deserve as well as strengthening our oversight with additional policies going forward. “The work the foundation does to save lives and unlock opportunities so people everywhere can lead healthier, more productive lives, is more important than ever,” he said. “We are committed to ensuring trust and transparency are never compromised.”

title: Steve Davison obituary
summary: Other lives: Trade union executive and tutor who was chair of the executive council of Amicus
body: My friend Steve Davison, who has died aged 71, was chair of the executive council of the Amicus trade union from 2003 to 2007. He also played a key role in the creation of the first global union, Workers Uniting, which consists of Amicus, with 1.2 million members, and the 800,000 strong United Steelworkers of the USA and Canada. In addition he was influential in the formation of the UKs biggest union, Unite, through a merger in 2007 between Amicus and the TGWU. During his time in those high-level union roles Steve worked as a tutor at the Trade Union Studies Centre in Bradford, which he ended up running for a number of years until its closure. Steve was born in Alston, Cumbria, to James, a car mechanic, and Audrey (nee Fawcett), a district nurse. He attended Samuel King’s school in Alston and then left Cumbria in 1973 to study history and politics at Bradford University. Settling in West Yorkshire afterwards, his first job was an engineer at Anderton International, a Bradford engineering company, where he met Diane Maude, who worked on the production line there. They were married in 1980. Having joined the engineers’ union, AEU, he soon stepped up to be a shop steward and then a factory convener, while also becoming active in the Labour party and Trades Council in Keighley. In the mid-1980s he was made redundant by Anderton, and ended up on an employment blacklist due to his trade union activities – a common experience in those days in the engineering and construction industries. As a result he took various poorly paid short term jobs, including office work in London, before finding a post at the Trade Union Studies Centre in Bradford, where he taught basic courses for union members as well as higher level diploma units for union reps. By the 90s he was also running the centre. After it closed in 2015 due to funding cuts, he took on freelance tutoring work for individual unions who had their own education programmes, and he also did some teaching on international courses, notably with unions from Norway. A committed internationalist, Steve visited Bangladesh for Amicus and Workers Uniting, helping shipbreakers there to unionise and to improve their dangerous working conditions. As part of visiting Amicus delegations he addressed the Venezuelan parliament in 2017 and met the president of Bolivia, Evo Morales, in 2008. He is survived by Diane, their children, Jennie and James, three grandchildren and his sisters Val and Judith.

title: Andy Burnham urges cabinet to examine ‘all possible ways’ to tackle cost of living crisis
summary: As new prime minister briefs cabinet on ‘cost of living government’, critics ask where money will come from
body: Andy Burnham is under mounting pressure to explain how he will pay for his policy pledges as he implored his new cabinet to examine “all possible ways” to tackle the cost of living crisis. On his first full day in power, the prime minister faced questions about how he would fund the removal of VAT from electricity bills and an expected uplift in defence spending – an issue that led his new chancellor, John Healey, to resign as Keir Starmer’s defence secretary last month. Sources say measures to ease the cost of living will include reducing the national bus fare cap by a third, from £3 to £2, with the prime minister set to announce the move on Wednesday. The Guardian has also learned Burnham plans to accelerate the timetable for implementing his promise to roll out a national care service, a move that could cost further money before the election. Gathering his cabinet in Downing Street for the first time, the prime minister told ministers his would be a “cost of living government”, adding that his priority would be to make life cheaper for voters – albeit within existing borrowing rules. “What can we do just to take that little bit of pressure off people’s shoulders, just to give them that little extra sense that help is coming so that they can have that bit of hope that things are getting better?” he asked colleagues. “And that’s the mission that we’ve got to set ourselves together.” He asked his colleagues to look into what their department could do to ease cost of living pressures, both “big and small”. “We need to be a cost of living government, getting that cost of living down, looking at all possible ways of doing that.” Burnham began the process on Tuesday morning with an announcement that VAT would be removed from electricity bills from 1 October, reducing the electricity price cap by £45 at a total cost of £850m next year. No 10 has ruled out using defence bonds to pay for higher military spending. The move came alongside major changes to Whitehall, which will see the creation of a powerful new Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet and hand responsibility for the UK’s growth strategy to the new No 10 North, which will be overseen by Louise Haigh, Burnham’s de facto deputy prime minister. Burnham’s promise to pay for the VAT cut by scrapping the government’s digital ID scheme was immediately called into question, however, by Darren Jones, the minister in charge of the policy until recently, who said the money for that scheme had not yet been identified. “The DigitalID program was unfunded,” he posted on X on Tuesday. “The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget.” Downing Street said on Tuesday that the tax cut was funded for the next financial year, but money would have to be found in future years from existing departmental budgets, suggesting spending cuts to come. No 10 has also ruled out using defence bonds to pay for higher military spending. The intervention from Jones came amid wider disquiet among allies of Starmer over Burnham’s pledge to end factional infighting, after more than half of the outgoing prime minister’s cabinet were removed or demoted in Monday’s reshuffle. One outgoing official said: “It’s less a purge of Starmerites than getting rid of people who were loyal to a Labour leader, while promoting people who were serially disloyal. That’s a dangerous message to send your MPs.” Burnham is set to continue making ministerial appointments this week, including giving the Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar a seat in the Lords and a job in the business department. Sarwar was the first major Labour figure to call for Keir Starmer to resign as prime minister earlier this year. Healey is looking at longer-term plans to cut the cost of living as part of a budget to be delivered later this year. A spokesperson for the prime minister added he had also ruled out using higher borrowing to pay for defence spending – something previously advocated within government by Healey. “It is not something we’re looking at,” they said. Sources have told the Guardian Burnham is now working on a major social care announcement in the coming weeks as he looks to begin laying the groundwork for a social care service before the election. This would involve bringing forward the review of social care by Louise Casey, who is due to report by 2028, but is now likely to do so as soon as next year. Those consulted on the policy say Burnham will give Casey scope to work up a range of options, including a new national service funded by taxes or a social insurance model that could see social care costs capped for a limited period of time. They admit, however, that any funding model is likely to trigger a political backlash with previous proposals having been abandoned amid a storm of controversy about the prospects of a new “death tax” being used to pay for them. Burnham told reporters this week: “I am ready to use some of my political capital on this issue … I would not want to leave office having done what Westminster has done, not just for 15 years – actually, I think you’re getting close to 30 years. “It’s not defensible. How many people have lost their homes and their savings in that time? We need to face it and fix it.” A spokesperson said the announcement would come “in due course”. Burnham admitted on Tuesday that he would face financial constraints as he looks to both ease the cost of living for voters and implement lasting social reforms. “We have got to show fiscal discipline, we have got to show our commitment to the fiscal rules is real,” he told his cabinet. As a result, allies say he has abandoned a nascent plan to raise the threshold at which workers start paying income taxe, which has been frozen for several years. The prime minister told the Times this week the issue had come up more than any other during the Makerfield byelection, but he is now understood to have dropped expensive plans to reverse the policy. This could have involved increasing the top rate of income tax to pay for it, but Burnham’s advisers believe this could be seen as a violation of Labour’s manifesto pledge not to raise income tax, national insurance or VAT. Burnham will spend the end of the week in Manchester, with the government encouraging civil servants working on growth to move to the city. A spokesperson said there would be relocation packages on offer for anyone choosing to do so.

title: Trump defends 50% tariffs on Canada, saying ‘they need us to survive’
summary: Mark Carney says Canada is ‘united’ in face of threats as leaders agree to talk before tariffs take effect next month
body: Donald Trump has defended his new 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, claiming the country cannot survive without the US. The White House announced the sweeping round of import duties – due to come into effect next month – on Monday, igniting fresh tensions between Washington and Ottawa. Mark Carney, the Canadian prime minister, said his country was “united” in the face of such threats. He spoke with Trump earlier on Tuesday. “Canada has been very, very tough on us over the years, for many years, and no other president’s done anything about it,” the US president said in the Oval Office. “In all fairness to them, they need us to survive. Without us, there’s no way they can survive.” Earlier this week, the White House said that the new levies were because Canada has unfairly discriminated against US cars, alcohol and dairy products. The tariffs will go into effect in 30 days, leaving time for possible discussions between the two countries. Carney told reporters: “We agreed to intensify negotiations in the coming weeks, and the team and myself look forward to doing that.” The tariffs will hit a wide range of products, the White House said, including wine, hockey sticks and cement. They also include goods previously protected from import taxes under the United States-Mexico-Canada (USMCA) agreement. Trump told members of the press that the latest round of tariffs was not in response to the Canadian wildfire smoke that spread across North America last week. On Friday, Trump blasted Canada for its response to the persistent wildfires and the smoke that affected about 109 million people across the midwest, mid-Atlantic and north-east. “The cost is incalculable!” Trump raged on social media. “This is Willful Negligence, and becoming a yearly occurrence, costing the United States Billions of Dollars, which cost of this pollution must of necessity be added to the TARIFFS Canada is currently paying.” However, the president told reporters on Tuesday that his administration was looking at those tariffs “separately”.

title: Pete Hegseth faces questions over $87.6bn additional funding for Pentagon
summary: Defense secretary and chair of the joint chiefs face Senate appropriations committee to continue Iran war effort
body: The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, walked into the Senate on Tuesday to defend the Trump administration’s $87.6bn supplemental funding request, facing lawmakers furious that the US-Israel war on Iran is costing far more than the White House has admitted. Hegseth and the chair of the joint chiefs, Gen Dan Caine, faced the Senate appropriations committee, chaired by Susan Collins of Maine, as the Pentagon said it was running low on cash to keep the fight going. Of the total request, $67.1bn of the request is earmarked specifically to keep the war effort funded. It was the pair’s first sustained public questioning since a brief ceasefire collapsed and the war roared back to life. At least two US soldiers were killed in an Iranian attack on Friday, and another died in Iraq destroying an Iranian drone, pushing the American death toll since February to 17. Nearly 100 troops have been wounded in the conflict, officials confirm, though most injuries were minor. Patty Murray, the committee’sDemocratic vice-chair, opened by demanding to know what the war had actually achieved, accusing the administration of shifting its rationale by the week. “We hear a different rationale for this war every few days,” she said, noting the justification had moved from preempting threats that “did not exist” to regime change, then to dismantling Iran’s nuclear programme and navy, and now, she said, to simply reopening the strait of Hormuz, a waterway that was “fully open, toll-free, before this war began”. Murray argued that Donald Trump had, “by any objective measure, failed to achieve his goals”, warning that depleted US munitions stockpiles would take years to rebuild and that the country’s global military posture had been left weaker, not stronger, by the war. Hegseth, whose testimony was interrupted by protesters a number of times, instead cast the funding request as the price of deterrence rather than admitting failure, and framed the Pentagon’s overhaul under the US president as a break from what he described as years of slow bureaucracy. “The best way to create peace is to prepare for war, to deter it,” Hegseth told the panel, arguing the department was “building a force so capable that it would be absurd for anyone to challenge it”. Hegseth said the $21bn readiness portion of the request would cover military pay, replace equipment used in recent operations and stabilize fuel shortfalls, while the $46bn capability portion would expand production of “solid rocket motors, JDAMs [joint direct attack munitions], hypersonics and counter-drone capabilities”. Without the funds, he warned, the department faced “critical shortfalls” that threaten its ability to pay troops and replenish munitions, and he cautioned that failing to fund the Pentagon at $1.5tn was “the greatest threat that our nation faces”. Some Democrats, including Chris Coons of Delaware, plan to direct criticism at Russell Vought, director of office of management and budget, who is not testifying but who earlier told US Congress that the war’s running cost was about $30bn, a figured largely believed by experts to be a severe undercount. Hegseth and Caine also face potential threats from within their own party. Collins and Alaska’s Lisa Murkowski, two prominent Republicans, have both crossed the aisle to back war-powers resolutions aimed at reining in the president’s authority over the conflict, and Collins has previously voiced frustration that the administration’s strategy for the war seems to shift almost daily. House representatives have separately accused the Pentagon of stonewalling on basic figures: Betty McCollum, the top Democrat on the House defense appropriations panel, said the department has missed a deadline to detail war costs and has not disclosed how many precision munitions remain after the military burned through thousands of them in Iran.

title: Anas Sarwar reportedly set to quit as Scottish Labour leader to be minister in Andy Burnham’s government – as it happened
summary: Sarwar expected to be elevated to Lords to take up a ministerial post at revamped Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade
body: Andy Burnham has told the first meeting of his new cabinet that they needed to examine “all possible ways” to help families struggling with high costs. Andy Burnham has created a powerful new prime minister’s department taking in parts of the Cabinet Office, Treasury and housing ministry to become the “dominant driver of the UK’s economy”. He has created the Office of the Prime Minister and Cabinet, to be led by his ally and enforcer Louise Haigh, which will oversee the No 10 operation in London, the forthcoming No 10 North in Manchester and parts of the Cabinet Office. Wes Streeting said Andy Burnham’s commitment to defence “will be even more clear in the coming months” as he indicated that military budgets would rise by over £25bn to hit a Nato target in 2035. The new defence secretary said the UK would “stick by” commitments it made to its allies – a reference to a pledge to dramatically lift spending to 3.5% of GDP in 2035 from its current level of 2.6%. The proportion of ministers in the newly-formed cabinet who were educated in state schools has fallen following Andy Burnham’s shake-up, according to analysis by the Sutton Trust social mobility charity. The trust calculated that 72% of the new cabinet went to state comprehensives while 24% attended private schools. Yvette Cooper will have to pay NHS doctors more or risk them staging more strikes, the British Medical Association (BMA) warned the new health secretary. Keir Starmer’s allies have questioned Andy Burnham’s pledge to end factional infighting after he removed or demoted more than half of the outgoing prime minister’s cabinet in his first reshuffle. “This was a deeply factional cabinet reshuffle,” one said. “The idea it was anything else is ridiculous.” Another former official said: “Becoming leader of a party involves having to promise things to a lot of different people, and you can see that in some appointments. What was surprising was the decision to get rid of so many impressive people.” Andy Burnham has dismissed the prospect of “further referendums” in his calls with the leaders of Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. Anas Sarwar is set to quit as Scottish Labour leader to be a minister in Andy Burnham’s government, according to reports. Sarwar is expected to be elevated to the Lords to take up a ministerial post at the revamped Department for Business, Innovation, Science and Trade, headed by Jonathan Reynolds, according to media including the Scottish Sun. On the frequent occasions that John Healey would travel abroad as defence secretary, he was so determined to keep working he would get the crew to tether a desk to the inside of his military aircraft so he could read his papers. His diligence is one reason why Andy Burnham, the new prime minister, made Healey his unexpected pick for chancellor on Monday. Allies say others include their shared commitment to re-industrialisation and devolution. After an occasionally vicious briefing war between allies of other potential candidates, the hardworking Healey is seen as a safe choice who can unite a fractured party while not scaring the markets. But many who know him say the new chancellor is more ideologically driven than he may seem, and that Burnham will come unstuck if he treats his most senior minister as little more than a factotum. “This is being billed as equivalent to Gordon Brown appointing Alistair Darling,” said one Labour official, referring to the former prime minister’s choice of chancellor, who was initially seen as someone who would simply do the bidding of No 10. “But it turned out Darling had views of his own which did not always chime with Brown’s – Andy has to avoid making the same mistake.” The prime minister told his new cabinet in its first meeting that the government will be a “cost-of-living government” and needs to examine “all possible ways” to help families struggling with high costs. Keir Starmer’s allies have questioned Andy Burnham’s pledge to end factional infighting after he removed or demoted more than half of the outgoing prime minister’s cabinet in his first reshuffle. “This was a deeply factional cabinet reshuffle,” one said. “The idea it was anything else is ridiculous.” Another former official said: “Becoming leader of a party involves having to promise things to a lot of different people, and you can see that in some appointments. What was surprising was the decision to get rid of so many impressive people.” Others are more phlegmatic, saying it was obvious that a new PM would want to bring in his own people and that the change was handled sensitively, with outgoing ministers being called directly by Burnham. Their special advisers – who usually go when their boss does – have also been given a chance to discuss their future with Alison Phillips, Burnham’s deputy chief of staff. The overall mood for the outgoing team has tended to be more reflective than bitter, after they had a month to digest Starmer’s decision to stand down, paving the way for a Burnham succession. A bid by the Conservatives to debate Andy Burnham’s policy agenda in the House of Lords before peers rise for summer recess has been blocked. The Tories had called for greater scrutiny of the new Prime Minister’s plans and accused him of “bending over backwards to avoid telling Parliament what his plans are”. However, peers voted 173 to 234, majority 61, to reject a motion which would have seen one of Mr Burnham’s new ministers summoned to the red benches on Wednesday. Leader of the House of Lords Baroness Smith of Basildon said the government will “welcome scrutiny”, but added: “In politics, as in life, patience is a virtue. “This is day two of a new Prime Minister. The Government is still being formed.” The new justice secretary Alex Norris has been urged to abandon controversial jury trial reforms, with Downing Street declining to say if Andy Burnham supported the plans. Shadow justice secretary Nick Timothy urged him not to press ahead with the “reckless plans” put forward by his predecessor David Lammy to tackle the backlog of court cases. Downing Street did not say if the new prime minister would remove the plans from the Courts and Tribunals Bill. The PM’s official spokesman told reporters: “I’m not going to get into speculation over possible policy positions of every subject today. “The Prime Minister has said before that he understands the pressure on the courts and the impact of thousands of victims waiting years for justice, and that this is not acceptable. But we will update in due course on that.” Jordan Bardella, the president of France’s far right National Rally, is to be a keynote speaker at this year’s Reform UK annual conference. In a sign of deepening links between the two parties, Bardella’s speech is expected to lay out how the two parties would work together in office. Reform UK’s leader, Nigel Farage, said: “I am pleased to welcome Jordan to our Party Conference. He represents a new generation of political leaders who are standing up for their countries, their borders and their people. “I look forward to discussing how France and Britain can co-operate to finally stop the boats once and for all.” Farage and Bardella met last year, discussing small boat crossings and energy policy. Bardella had been tipped to be the National Rally’s candidate in the upcoming French presidential elections but its long-time leader, Marine Le Pen, has recently had the path cleared for her to stand after a French appeal court shorted a ban on her holding public office. Downing Street has insisted Andy Burnham’s plan to cut VAT from electricity bills is fully funded. The PM’s official spokesman said: “It’s fully funded and will come into effect from October the first, and it applies and is funded for this financial year.” The Government previously said the £1.8 billion cost of the digital ID scheme over three years had been “scored” by the Office for Budget Responsibility, meaning the money would have had to be found. The spokesman was asked about the scoring of the digital ID programme and which funding is to be shifted from it for the VAT cut, as the Office for Budget Responsibility previously said that money for digital ID still needed to be found. Put to him that the cut was being funded through unidentified savings, he said: “What we’re doing is we’re funding this through the cancellation of the digital ID programme. “Departments were going to have to fund the Digital ID programme through their existing budget reprioritisation. “Departments will now direct these savings towards this.” Chris O’Shea, group chief executive of British Gas owner Centrica, said it is “encouraging to see the new Prime Minister move quickly” to cut household energy bills after announcing an £850 million cut to VAT on electricity bills. Andy Burnham’s conversation with Donald Trump was “extremely warm” and lasted around 20 minutes, Downing Street has said. The Prime Minister’s spokesman told reporters: “It was an extremely warm call and I think that’s reflected in both sides’ read-outs.” Andy Burnham insists No 10 North is “not a gimmick” and the “time for resisting devolution was over” in his first Cabinet meeting. The PM’s spokesman said: “Turning to Number 10 North, the Prime Minister said it was not a gimmick. “He said the time for resisting devolution was over, and it was very clear that this was the direction the Government was heading in to speed up growth and delivery.” He said this was “the urgent principle” of this government. Andy Burnham urged his Cabinet to think every day about how to ease the pressure on household budgets for the public. In a readout of the first Cabinet meeting, the PM’s official spokesman said: “Today’s cabinet reflected a sense of a united team, a new sense of energy, and a second chance at a first impression.” He said Burnham opened by congratulating his Cabinet and telling them they were a “good team from all backgrounds that reflected the country they served”, adding it…

title: Saxophonist Plas Johnson, known for solo on The Pink Panther Theme, dies aged 94
summary: Jazz saxophonist – who recorded with artists Frank Sinatra, BB King and more – died in Los Angeles, reports say
body: The US jazz saxophonist Plas Johnson, best known for his solo riff on the timeless classic The Pink Panther Theme, has died at the age of 94, according to reports. The native of Donaldsonville, Louisiana, died on 15 July, according to a report from the Los Angeles jazz radio station KKJZ. Johnson’s hometown newspaper, the Donaldsonville Chief, added in a separate report that he died in Granada Hills, a neighborhood in Los Angeles. Social media posts from users identifying themselves as Louisiana family members of the musician also shared word of Johnson’s death. “My cousin Mr Plas Johnson passed away,” one Donaldsonville-based Facebook user said on 17 July. The post added: “RIP Cuz.” He was most famous for his performance on The Pink Panther Theme, which he recorded in 1963 alongside the late US composer Henry Mancini. The score to that movie was later ranked 20th on the American Film Institute’s list of greatest film scores ever. It also won three Grammy awards and was nominated for an Oscar for best original score at the 37th Academy awards. “We only did two takes, I think,” Johnson once said, according to the Donaldsonville Chief. “When we finished, everyone applauded, even the string players.” Johnson, who performed the classic’s tenor saxophone solo, added jokingly: “And that’s saying something … They never applaud for anything.” He began his musical career singing with his family before his father, who played both the banjo and saxophone, bought him a soprano saxophone. Johnson made his recording debut in 1950 alongside his brother, Ray, for DeLuxe Records before touring with the singer and pianist Charles Brown. After switching from soprano to alto and tenor saxophone, Johnson moved to Los Angeles in 1954, where he became a session musician, recording with artists who, beside Mancini, included BB King, Johnny Otis, Frank Sinatra, Marvin Gaye and Nat King Cole. “My solos always seemed to bring the record up another notch,” Johnson said. “I could do that in eight bars, I could do that in 12 bars and I was used to maybe do[ing] fills behind the vocalist after that,” he added. Johnson died six days shy of his 95th birthday, according to KKJZ and the Donaldsonville Chief.

title: ‘The explosion is what I remember’: man nearly sucked out of Ryanair plane recounts ordeal
summary: Exclusive: Ljubisa Karović tells Guardian the fact that he lived to tell the tale is nothing short of a miracle
body: It is the explosion just before he was nearly sucked out of the aircraft that Ljubisa Karović remembers. The thunderous noise of the window shattering that roused him from slumber in 11F, his window seat on the early morning Ryanair flight from Greece to Germany. Eleven days and 11 nights later, it remains the prelude “to the chaos, the catastrophe” that keeps him awake at night. “The explosion is what I remember,” says the Serbian entrepreneur in an interview with the Guardian. His head supported by a neck brace, his eyes staring into the middle distance, he adds: “It’s the noise before the chaos, the noise that is always there when I close my eyes to sleep.” Pain still haunts Ljubisa Karović, too, and it is easy to understand why. The burns and bruises livid on his right arm, ascending to his armpit and chest, are testimony to a fact that few will forget: that the 61-year-old was sucked out of an aircraft window – dislodged mid-flight in circumstances still under investigation – and survived. “I am lucky,” he says with some understatement as he swats a fly on the porch of the unassuming villa he has rented in Katerini, the booming coastal resort outside Thessaloniki. “I don’t remember much and my head and neck still hurt but I am still alive. It could have been the seatbelt, the fact that I was still strapped in. But maybe it is fate. I believe in God and I thank him every day.” Barely 100 hours have elapsed since Karović was discharged from hospital. The shock, he says, was overwhelming but the fact that he can tell the tale at all is nothing short of a miracle. His Greek lawyer, Vasilis Tsiaras, describes the wiry father of two as “being alive by mistake”. “Ljubisa dipped in and out of death,” he says. “When the cabin lost pressure he was hurled out of the window from his chest up and immediately lost consciousness. He was on that plane heading to Germany to celebrate his brother’s birthday. Instead, at 15,000ft he ended up with his head and right arm outside the aircraft as it moved at a speed of more than 600km/h. Imagine that! He looked at death, that other world, and came back.” Passengers on flight FR1879, including Karović’s wife, Svetlana, seated in 14C, three rows behind her husband, still have vivid recollections of the moment the window smashed, the oxygen masks coming down, the commotion that ensued as bottles and other objects flew through the air and fellow passengers grabbing Ljubisa, desperately trying to hold him in place. “I rushed over too. None of the cabin crew helped. It was the passengers,” she says. “One man, I think he was an Albanian, was incredible. He did all he could to bring him in and then tried to block the window, first with a bag that was immediately sucked out and then with a suitcase, which worked. We are eternally grateful to him.” The unbearable moments of seeing her husband “half in and out of the plane” are thought to have lasted between one-and-a-half and two minutes. “They tell me I fainted twice and again inside the plane and that my face was briefly swollen and deformed because of the pressure, the rush of air,” says the softly spoken Karović. “My wife reminds me about that.” Ryanair has defended the performance of its staff saying cabin crew did a “phenomenal job”. Tsiaras, the lawyer, takes issue with this. While Karović’s son, Danjell, who booked the flight, has been contacted by the company, neither Ljubisa nor Svetlana have been called or received any assistance or support, he says. “We have no complaint with the pilot, who followed the safety protocols and dealt with the situation very well,” adds Tsiaras referring to the rapid descent they are forced to make in the event of a plane losing cabin pressure. “My client fainted two or three times, once outside the aircraft when he was partially sucked out of the window, and twice inside when, again, he lost consciousness. At no point did the cabin crew offer to help.” Ryanair disputed that claim, saying that during the process of depressurisation and rapid decent to lower altitude, all passengers and cabin crew are required to fasten seatbelts and put on oxygen masks. “As soon as the aircraft descended to a safe altitude our cabin crew attended to and assisted Mr Karović, moving him to alternate seats (beside his wife) and arranged for a doctor onboard to sit with them until the aircraft landed safely back in Thessaloniki. They also arranged to have medical assistance standing by on landing in Thessaloniki,” the airline said in a statement to the Guardian. Citing witness testimony, Tsiaras claimed the cabin crew personnel were paralysed by fear, perhaps brought on by the belief that with the dramatic loss in altitude the aircraft was about to crash. “Everyone has agreed that the crew was in a state of shock, afraid that the plane was about to crash. It was the other passengers who rallied and gave Ljubisa water,” he says. The owner of an aluminium factory based in Serbia that for years has exported goods far and wide, Karović, long a frequent flyer, recoils at the thought of travelling by plane again. Doctors have warned that recovery will be slow, ordering him to wear the neck brace for at least six weeks when they will determine if surgery is necessary. “There are images that come back, the noise of the explosion, the chaos, but I want to recover,” he says. “And, yes, there should be compensation too. Not only emails that have focused on rebooking the flight.” Asked if there is a message he wants to convey, he allows himself to break into a smile. “Always make sure to wear your seatbelt. I don’t want anyone to suffer what happened to me.” • This article was amended on 21 July 2026. An earlier version incorrectly said Ljubisa Karović is the only man to have been sucked out of an aircraft window and survived. In 1990, a British Airways pilot, Timothy Lancaster, also survived after being blasted halfway through a cockpit windscreen that blew out over Oxfordshire.

title: Fiscal hawk or big spender – what kind of chancellor will John Healey be?
summary: Diligent, popular, unexpected pick for the Treasury has been given a straitjacket – and the job of funding PM’s policy promises
body: On the frequent occasions that John Healey would travel abroad as defence secretary, he was so determined to keep working he would get the crew to tether a desk to the inside of his military aircraft so he could read his papers. His diligence is one reason why Andy Burnham, the new prime minister, made Healey his unexpected pick for chancellor on Monday. Allies say others include their shared commitment to re-industrialisation and devolution. After an occasionally vicious briefing war between allies of other potential candidates, the hardworking Healey is seen as a safe choice who can unite a fractured party while not scaring the markets. But many who know him say the new chancellor is more ideologically driven than he may seem, and that Burnham will come unstuck if he treats his most senior minister as little more than a factotum. “This is being billed as equivalent to Gordon Brown appointing Alistair Darling,” said one Labour official, referring to the former prime minister’s choice of chancellor, who was initially seen as someone who would simply do the bidding of No 10. “But it turned out Darling had views of his own which did not always chime with Brown’s – Andy has to avoid making the same mistake.” The decision to appoint Healey was arguably the most important Burnham made on his first day in Downing Street. The new chancellor will be tasked with finding money for the prime minister’s various policy promises, including higher defence spending, taking utilities into public control and finding ways to cut voters’ living costs. Burnham has promised not to raise income tax, VAT or national insurance, and to stick to the last government’s borrowing rules, placing his chancellor in the same straitjacket that hampered his predecessor, Rachel Reeves. Healey is in an almost unique position to take the tough decisions required while keeping Labour MPs onboard. Since entering parliament in 1997, he has largely avoided becoming attached to one faction or another. Although he served in the Treasury for five years while Brown was chancellor, he was always respected by the opposing Blairite camp. More recently, he has successfully bridged the widening gap between allies of the former prime minister Keir Starmer and those on the soft left agitating for his removal. Part of Healey’s ability to appeal to different wings of the party comes from his personal warmth. He will often involve relatively junior members of staff in policy and political discussions and, like his new boss in No 10, has an ability to remember names and details about people’s lives. David Walker, the bishop of Manchester, was one of the local Labour members who voted for him to be the Labour candidate for the seat of Wentworth in 1997 – over other rising stars such as Yvette Cooper – now health secretary. “One of the things that impressed us at the time was that John was committed to living in the community and putting down roots here,” said Walker. “He always made sure he turned up to community events and showed his face, even when he was busy with national politics.” Luke Pollard, his former ministerial colleague at the Ministry of Defence, called Healey “genuinely the finest and most decent man I’ve worked with since being elected”. As defence secretary, Healey earned a reputation as a strong ally of the armed forces, putting the repair of dilapidated military housing at the top of his list of priorities. To some in government however, this closeness to the military was a weakness. When he asked the Treasury for an extra £28bn to fund the defence investment plan (Dip), just months after negotiating a multi-year budget, Reeves was infuriated. Her allies said Healey had been hoodwinked by service chiefs into believing that last year’s strategic defence review was fully funded, only to have to go back and ask for more when it came to thrashing out the detail. Those close to the chancellor, however, say he needed more money for his budget because of the additional geopolitical commitments Starmer had made in the months after the defence review, including playing peacekeeping roles in Ukraine and the Gulf. One former government official said the row showed that far from being a fiscal hawk, as some have claimed, Healey would come down on the side of higher spending when presented with a policy dilemma. “He is definitely not a fiscal hawk or even a firm hand in the tiller as he’s being portrayed by some,” they said. “He is a big spender.” In the end, Healey resigned in protest just days before the Dip, which some of Starmer’s allies believe was part of a deal with Burnham to become his chancellor. Wes Streeting, Healey’s replacement as defence secretary, suggested additional money was also likely to be found from cuts elsewhere. “We understand the sacrifices that involves from other government departments,” he told a press conference during his first day in the job. The chancellor will now have to do the kind of adjudicating between departments that could, for the first time, make him unpopular with large parts of the parliamentary Labour party. The question is whether, when faced with a minister in sudden need of extra cash, as he himself once was, he loosens the purse strings or sticks to the discipline of his predecessor.
